content.problemStatement:
During a fight with the Joker, Batman's eyes lose the capability to distinguish between some pairs of colors.

Each color has an integer ID from 111 to NNN. There are MMM lists where each color belongs to exactly one list. Batman can distinguish colors belonging to different lists, but he cannot distinguish colors belonging to the same list.

Given a strip of LLL colors, find the different number of segments Batman will see as a result of his disability. Two positions of the strip are said to belong to the same segment if they are adjacent on the strip and Batman cannot distinguish their colors. See the sample explanation for clarity.


Input Format

The first line contains an integer TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line contain three integers NNN, MMM, and LLL - the number of colors, the number of lists, and the length of the strip, respectively.
Each of the next MMM lines describes a list. It begins with an integer KiK_iKi​, the length of the iii-th list, followed by KiK_iKi​ integers Ai1,Ai2,…,AiKiA_{i1}, A_{i2}, \ldots, A_{iK_i}Ai1​,Ai2​,…,AiKi​​ - the color IDs of the iii-th list.
The next line contains LLL integers S1,S2,…,SLS_1, S_2, \ldots, S_LS1​,S2​,…,SL​ - the color IDs of the strip.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line the answer to the problem.
constraints: ∑ Ki = N (each color belongs to exactly one list)
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- A line with three integers N, M and L – the number of colors, the number of lists and the length of the strip.
- M following lines describe the lists. The i‑th line starts with Ki, the size of the i‑th list, followed by Ki distinct integers denoting the color IDs belonging to that list. Every color from 1 to N appears in exactly one list.
- A line with L integers S1 … SL – the color IDs of the strip in order.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of segments Batman will see on the strip.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: each color belongs to a different list, so every position forms its own segment → 2 segments.
Test case 2: positions 1‑2 are different lists, positions 3‑4 are different lists, giving three segments.
Test case 3: all colors are from the same list, therefore the whole strip is a single segment.

hidden.label: edge case with long same‑list run

solution.approach: For each test case we first build an array listId[1…N] where listId[c] stores the index of the list that contains color c. This is done while reading the M lists – for every color read we set listId[color] = currentListIndex. Afterwards we read the strip. The number of visible segments equals 1 plus the number of positions i (2 ≤ i ≤ L) where listId[Si] differs from listId[Si‑1]. We simply scan the strip once, compare the list ids of consecutive colors and count the changes. The answer is 1 if L > 0, otherwise 0 (L is always ≥1 by constraints).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + L) per test case – building the mapping takes O(N) and scanning the strip takes O(L).
